news_summary: Today's technology news highlights innovative scientific discoveries and the impactful role of AI in enhancing human capabilities.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des découvertes scientifiques innovantes et le rôle déterminant de l'IA dans l'amélioration des capacités humaines.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los innovadores descubrimientos científicos y el importante papel que desempeña la inteligencia artificial en la mejora de las capacidades humanas.

articles.title: How I eavesdrop on frog conversations
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01621-6Billie Goolsby’s experience with hearing loss helped her to develop a robotic tadpole for deciphering the secret lives of amphibians.
articles.content:
Billie Goolsby (right) and Lauren O’Connell study parent–offspring communication in frogs.Credit: Zach Reddy

Working scientist profiles This article is part of an occasional series in which Nature profiles scientists with unusual career histories or outside interests.

When the tadpoles of some poison frogs talk to their parents, they don’t croak or sing. Instead, they speak in a language of vibration, performing a wriggling dance against their mother’s or father’s body. The parents somehow judge their offspring’s hunger from this vibration.

Scientists don’t yet know exactly how the tadpole vibrations translate into parental marching orders. But when Billie Goolsby started her PhD research at Stanford University in California in 2020, she felt uniquely equipped to investigate the question. Goolsby was born hard of hearing, and her mother spoke to her using a language that, like the amphibians’ communication, included touch.

A sit in the sauna can save endangered frogs

“Parent–offspring communication is often the most crucial and the primary interaction that social creatures have,” Goolsby says. “Parents really care about what their babies say.”

The various species of poison frog that Goolsby’s PhD adviser, Lauren O’Connell, studies in the laboratory care for their young in ways that might seem surprising. In many species, fathers give their newly hatched tadpoles piggybacks to pools of water, such as rainwater cupped in a leaf. A mimic poison frog (Ranitomeya imitator) father puts each of its 2–4 tadpoles in a separate pool and patrols the pools daily. If he stands in the water and feels a tadpole vibrating, he sings relentlessly to his mate until she comes to join him. The mother feels the tadpole’s jiggling for herself and decides whether she needs to lay an unfertilized egg for it to eat.

To try to decode a family’s conversation, Goolsby teamed up with Stanford engineers and built a one-of-a-kind robot that mimics a tadpole’s vibrations. Her project has begun to unlock the secrets of an amphibian language — and has also transformed how the scientists around her collaborate and communicate.

Goolsby calls her schoolteacher mother “a superhero”. When Goolsby was growing up, her mum worked out ways to communicate with her, such as using touch to guide her through a noisy crowd. She taught Goolsby to enunciate words by paying attention to how the sounds felt in her mouth. “Touch, to me, is much more salient” than it is to other people, Goolsby says.

When she moved from her small secondary school in Georgia to Boston University in Massachusetts, Goolsby struggled in the large lecture classes. “I told my chemistry professor that I was hard of hearing, but I really wanted to stay in science,” she recalls. “And he said to me, ‘people like you don’t stay in science or medicine’.”

Mimic poison frog tadpoles communicate their needs by wiggling.Credit: Zach Reddy

During American Sign Language (ASL) classes in the university’s Deaf Studies programme, however, Goolsby met faculty members who were Deaf or hard of hearing and she got a different message, she says: “You can do anything. And you can do it as your full self.” She learnt to tell people what she needed, such as asking people to stay close and face her while speaking.

While studying biology, Goolsby started thinking about the “adaptive ways that animals succeed in their environments”. For example, she wondered, how do the members of a frog family — with land-based parents and aquatic babies — speak to one another?

TadBot is born

Goolsby looked for ways to answer this question when she started in O’Connell’s lab. If she had a tool that could artificially imitate a tadpole’s dance, she could control the stimulus the frog parents received, and decode the signal that tells them to feed their young. But no such tool existed.

My mission to make life more user friendly for the disability community

Goolsby sent an e-mail to the Biomimetics and Dexterous Manipulation laboratory at Stanford, which had made devices inspired by biology, including a robot that climbs walls like a gecko. What would they think, she asked, about building a robotic tadpole?

It would have to be tiny, noiseless and work underwater and it would need to ‘wiggle’ similarly to a real tadpole. “I was a little bit nervous presenting this to the robotics folks,” Goolsby says. “This is a long list of demands.”

But Tony Chen, the senior graduate student who answered her e-mail, says the project immediately grabbed his attention. “It’s such an interesting engineering challenge.”

The robot would sit inside a small, artificial water pool — a film canister, actually — in its parents’ enclosure. Chen considered powering the robot with pneumatics, pushing air through it, but that would produce bubbles. He also thought about using hydraulics to power the robot with water. But that risked raising the canister’s water level too high.

The TadBot robotic tadpole designed by engineers at Stanford University is able to closely mimic a real tadpole’s hunger vibrations. Credit: Zach Reddy

He settled on a motor situated outside the enclosure (where its noise and pulsations wouldn’t bother the frogs or interfere with their signalling), connected by a long cord to the robot’s wiggling mechanism. Chen covered the robot’s two-centimetre-long body with dark-grey silicone to mimic the skin of a real tadpole.

He gained a new appreciation for the difficulties of animal-behaviour research: many variables couldn’t be controlled, the experiments took a long time and the test subjects couldn’t fill out a survey afterwards. After each trial of their robot, the researchers came together to talk about what had or hadn’t worked.

For instance, when the first iteration of TadBot danced for its parents, something got lost in translation. The frog fathers saw the robotic tadpole wiggling and, bizarrely, climbed on top of it. “The frogs were riding this tadpole like a bull,” O’Connell says, laughing.

That robot model was too big and clunky, O’Connell says, and the vibrations weren’t right. The researchers realized the tadpole language was more subtle than they’d thought.

Ripples of influence

A breakthrough didn’t come until Goolsby chatted at a conference with sensory biologist Loranzie Rogers, who was at the time doing his own PhD research at the University of Washington in Seattle on how toadfish (which are fish, not amphibians) respond to sounds. Rogers offered to visit Goolsby at Stanford and bring his equipment along.

Once Rogers had helped the researchers to measure the amplitude and frequency of real tadpoles’ begging vibrations, “we were able to tune our TadBot to get that exact signal”, Goolsby says. Finally, her robot was up and shimmying.

I’m a lip-reading scientist: here’s how I can discuss science with you

Once TadBot was vibrating in the right way, the frog parents visited it more often. The scientists had cracked at least part of the code. But would the frog mum and dad care for their robotic baby as they would a real one?
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific and technological breakthrough where a robotic tadpole was developed to decode amphibian communication, which has broad implications for understanding animal behavior and parent-offspring interactions. The story highlights an inspiring real-world impact involving interdisciplinary collaboration and innovative engineering, with meaningful benefits for biological research and conservation.
articles.personality_title: Scientists build a robotic tadpole to understand frog family talks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some poison frog tadpoles communicate with their parents not by croaking but by vibrating their bodies. Parents use these vibrations to understand when their young are hungry, but scientists didn’t know exactly how this worked.

**What happened** – Billie Goolsby, a scientist born hard of hearing, teamed up with engineers at Stanford University to create a tiny robot called TadBot. This robot mimics the vibrations of real tadpoles. It can wiggle underwater quietly and precisely, allowing researchers to study how frog parents respond to their babies’ signals.

**Impact** – TadBot helps scientists decode a hidden language between frog parents and their tadpoles. This is important because it reveals how animals communicate in ways humans can’t easily detect. The project also shows how combining biology, engineering, and personal experience can lead to new discoveries.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use TadBot to test different vibration patterns to learn exactly what signals trigger parental care. This could improve understanding of animal behavior and help protect endangered frog species by better knowing their needs.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A robotic tadpole built by a hard-of-hearing scientist is helping decode the secret vibration language frogs use to care for their young.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un robot têtard aide à comprendre la communication familiale des grenouilles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certains têtards de grenouilles venimeuses communiquent avec leurs parents non pas en coassant, mais en vibrant leur corps. Les parents utilisent ces vibrations pour savoir quand leurs petits ont faim, mais les scientifiques ne comprenaient pas encore comment cela fonctionnait exactement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Billie Goolsby, une scientifique malentendante, a collaboré avec des ingénieurs de l’Université Stanford pour créer un petit robot appelé TadBot. Ce robot imite les vibrations des vrais têtards. Il peut bouger sous l’eau, silencieusement et précisément, ce qui permet aux chercheurs d’étudier comment les parents grenouilles réagissent aux signaux de leurs petits.

**Impact** – TadBot aide les scientifiques à décoder un langage caché entre les parents grenouilles et leurs têtards. Cela est important parce que cela révèle comment les animaux communiquent de façons que les humains ne peuvent pas facilement détecter. Ce projet montre aussi comment la biologie, l’ingénierie et l’expérience personnelle peuvent mener à de nouvelles découvertes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront TadBot pour tester différents types de vibrations afin de comprendre précisément quels signaux provoquent les soins parentaux. Cela pourrait améliorer la connaissance du comportement animal et aider à protéger les espèces de grenouilles menacées.

**Une phrase à retenir** – Un robot têtard conçu par une scientifique malentendante aide à déchiffrer le langage secret des vibrations que les grenouilles utilisent pour s’occuper de leurs petits.
articles.personality_title_es: Un robot renacuajo ayuda a entender el lenguaje familiar de las ranas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunos renacuajos de ranas venenosas se comunican con sus padres no croando, sino vibrando sus cuerpos. Los padres usan estas vibraciones para saber cuándo sus crías tienen hambre, pero los científicos no entendían exactamente cómo funcionaba.

**Qué pasó** – Billie Goolsby, una científica con discapacidad auditiva, trabajó con ingenieros de la Universidad de Stanford para crear un pequeño robot llamado TadBot. Este robot imita las vibraciones de los renacuajos reales. Puede moverse bajo el agua, silenciosamente y con precisión, lo que permite a los investigadores estudiar cómo reaccionan los padres rana a las señales de sus crías.

**Impacto** – TadBot ayuda a los científicos a descifrar un lenguaje oculto entre padres y renacuajos. Esto es importante porque muestra cómo los animales se comunican de formas que los humanos no pueden detectar fácilmente. El proyecto también demuestra cómo la biología, la ingeniería y la experiencia personal pueden llevar a nuevos descubrimientos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán TadBot para probar diferentes patrones de vibración y entender qué señales activan el cuidado parental. Esto podría mejorar el conocimiento del comportamiento animal y ayudar a proteger especies de ranas en peligro.

**Una frase para recordar** – Un robot renacuajo creado por una científica con discapacidad auditiva está ayudando a descifrar el lenguaje secreto de vibraciones que usan las ranas para cuidar a sus crías.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle scene of a small, dark-grey robotic tadpole softly vibrating inside a clear water pool held by a large, protective silhouette of a poison frog parent, both surrounded by calm water ripples symbolizing communication, rendered as a warm, detailed painting with natural muted greens, browns, and soft greys.

articles.title: AI won’t replace you but someone using AI might
articles.summary: Generative AI is transforming the workplace faster than ever, but new research from the University of Vaasa suggests the biggest threat may not be AI itself — it’s falling behind in learning how to use it. Researcher Zhe Zhu found that employees who see tools like ChatGPT and Gemini as helpful collaborators rather than job-stealing rivals tend to be more engaged, adaptable, and optimistic about their careers.
articles.content:
The rapid rise of generative artificial intelligence has fueled growing fears about job loss and the future of work. However, new research from the University of Vaasa in Finland suggests AI may actually help employees become more engaged and adaptable when they view the technology as a useful partner rather than a threat.

In his doctoral dissertation, researcher Zhe Zhu explored how generative AI tools such as ChatGPT and Gemini are changing workplaces and influencing employees' experiences on the job. The research focused on both organizational decision making and the human side of AI adoption.

Many workers remain concerned about losing control or becoming less relevant as AI tools spread through the workplace. Yet Zhu's findings suggest that these concerns can sometimes motivate employees to learn new skills and use AI more effectively to strengthen their careers.

"As NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang has pointed out, workers are not simply being replaced by AI, but by those who have learned to use GenAI to work more effectively. The workers that perceive GenAI more positively are also more engaged and adaptable in their careers," notes Zhu.

Why Trust in AI Matters

According to the research, trust plays a major role in determining whether AI helps or harms employees and organizations. Workers who trust AI too much may accept inaccurate information without questioning it, while employees who distrust the technology completely may overlook its potential advantages.

Zhu argues that organizations must carefully manage this balance as AI becomes part of everyday workflows.

Building an AI Ready Workplace

The study found that successful AI adoption depends less on the technology itself and more on how organizations implement it. Companies need to address ethical concerns, data privacy, and responsible governance while integrating AI into their operations.

"Organizations should follow a strategic roadmap to align the technology with their goals and build ecosystems with industry and academic partners. My research proposes an eight-step framework that guides organizations in moving from experimentation toward a more integrated and purposeful use of GenAI," states Zhu.

Zhu believes workplaces are steadily moving toward an AI native future where AI systems are fully woven into daily tasks and processes rather than functioning as separate tools.

AI Will Change Jobs and Create New Industries

The research also points to broader economic changes driven by AI. While some jobs may disappear, Zhu says entirely new industries and career paths are likely to emerge around AI infrastructure, data centers, and digital services.

"We are in a new industrial revolution. Some jobs will disappear, but new forms of work and entirely new industries will also emerge around AI infrastructure, data centers, and digital services. Instead of fearing the technology, employees should learn how to use it critically and develop their skills alongside it," says Zhu.

Dissertation

Zhu, Zhe (2026) Generative Artificial Intelligence in Organizations: Strategic Decisions and Human Adaptations. Acta Wasaensia 586. Doctoral dissertation. University of Vaasa.

Zhe Zhu was born in 1982 in China. He earned a Master's degree in Industrial Systems Analytics from the University of Vaasa in 2021 and currently works as a Grant Funded Researcher at the university in the field of Information Systems Science.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on research showing that generative AI, when embraced positively by employees, can enhance engagement, adaptability, and career optimism, highlighting a constructive approach to AI adoption in workplaces. It discusses broad societal implications including workforce transformation, new industry creation, and strategic organizational frameworks for responsible AI use, providing substantial context and significance.
articles.personality_title: Using AI well, not AI itself, shapes the future of work
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools like ChatGPT and Gemini are quickly changing workplaces. Many workers worry about losing their jobs or control as AI becomes more common.

**What happened** – Research from the University of Vaasa in Finland, led by Zhe Zhu, studied how employees react to AI in their jobs. The study found that workers who see AI as a helpful partner tend to be more engaged, adaptable, and positive about their careers. Zhu also highlighted that the real risk is not AI replacing jobs, but people falling behind those who learn to use AI effectively.

**Impact** – This research shows that trusting AI carefully and learning to work with it can improve job satisfaction and career growth. It also points out that companies need to guide AI use responsibly, balancing trust and caution. Finally, AI is expected to create new industries and job types, offering fresh opportunities rather than only threats.

**What's next step** – Organizations are encouraged to follow strategic plans for AI adoption, addressing ethics, privacy, and training. Workers should focus on building skills alongside AI tools. The workplace is moving toward an AI-native future where AI is part of everyday tasks.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Embracing AI as a helpful tool and learning to use it well can lead to better jobs and new career paths, rather than job loss.
articles.personality_title_fr: Bien utiliser l’IA, pas l’IA elle-même, façonne l’avenir du travail
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les outils d’intelligence artificielle générative (IA) comme ChatGPT et Gemini transforment rapidement les lieux de travail. Beaucoup de salariés craignent de perdre leur emploi ou le contrôle face à cette technologie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une recherche menée par l’Université de Vaasa en Finlande, dirigée par Zhe Zhu, a étudié comment les employés vivent l’arrivée de l’IA au travail. L’étude montre que ceux qui considèrent l’IA comme un partenaire utile sont plus engagés, adaptables et optimistes pour leur carrière. Zhu souligne que le vrai risque n’est pas que l’IA remplace les emplois, mais que certains restent en arrière face à ceux qui apprennent à bien utiliser l’IA.

**Impact** – Cette recherche révèle qu’une confiance mesurée en l’IA et une bonne formation peuvent améliorer la satisfaction et la progression professionnelle. Elle insiste aussi sur le rôle des entreprises pour encadrer l’usage de l’IA de manière responsable. Enfin, l’IA devrait créer de nouveaux secteurs et emplois, offrant plus d’opportunités que de menaces.

**Prochaine étape** – Les organisations doivent suivre des plans stratégiques pour intégrer l’IA, en tenant compte de l’éthique, de la confidentialité et de la formation. Les salariés doivent développer leurs compétences avec les outils d’IA. Le futur du travail tend vers un environnement où l’IA est intégrée au quotidien.

**Phrase clé** – Considérer l’IA comme un outil utile et apprendre à s’en servir ouvre la voie à de meilleurs emplois et de nouvelles carrières, plutôt qu’à la perte d’emploi.
articles.personality_title_es: Usar bien la IA, no la IA en sí, define el futuro del trabajo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las herramientas de inteligencia artificial generativa como ChatGPT y Gemini están cambiando rápidamente los lugares de trabajo. Muchos empleados temen perder sus puestos o el control ante esta tecnología.

**Qué pasó** – Una investigación de la Universidad de Vaasa en Finlandia, dirigida por Zhe Zhu, estudió cómo los trabajadores perciben la llegada de la IA en sus empleos. El estudio encontró que quienes ven la IA como un compañero útil están más comprometidos, son más adaptables y tienen una visión más positiva de sus carreras. Zhu destacó que el verdadero riesgo no es que la IA reemplace empleos, sino que algunos queden rezagados frente a quienes aprenden a usarla bien.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación muestra que confiar con medida en la IA y capacitarse para usarla puede mejorar la satisfacción laboral y el desarrollo profesional. También subraya la importancia de que las empresas gestionen responsablemente el uso de la IA. Además, se espera que la IA cree nuevas industrias y trabajos, abriendo más oportunidades que amenazas.

**Próximo paso** – Las organizaciones deben seguir planes estratégicos para integrar la IA, considerando ética, privacidad y formación. Los trabajadores deben desarrollar habilidades junto con la IA. El futuro laboral se acerca a un entorno donde la IA está integrada en las tareas diarias.

**Frase clave** – Ver la IA como una herramienta útil y aprender a usarla bien puede conducir a mejores empleos y nuevas carreras, en lugar de pérdidas laborales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting showing a human silhouette and a friendly, glowing AI interface made of softly swirling light strands, working together harmoniously at a shared desk surrounded by floating, interconnected gears and digital data streams, symbolizing partnership, adaptability, and skill-building in a modern, AI-integrated workplace.

articles.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
articles.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.
articles.content:
Amber from Myanmar's Kachin region continues to reveal extraordinary glimpses into life during the age of dinosaurs. Preserved inside the 100 million-year-old resin are fossils from an ancient forest ecosystem that once supported a remarkable variety of creatures, including species never seen before.

Now, researchers at Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU) have identified a newly discovered true bug (Heteroptera) with one especially unusual feature. Its front legs ended in large claw-like structures that resemble the pincers of crabs. These appendages, known as chelae, function much like forceps and are extremely uncommon among insects.

Ancient Insect With Rare Crab-Like Claws

"Previously, such chelae were known from only three insect groups. This fossil therefore represents the fourth known case of these structures evolving independently in insects," explains Privatdozent Carolin Haug, zoologist at LMU's Faculty of Biology.

Haug and her colleagues worked with researchers from the University of Rostock and the University of Oulu in Finland to study the fossil in detail. Using micro-computed tomography, the team created highly detailed 3D images that allowed them to examine the insect's anatomy. Their findings were published in the journal Insects.

The scientists also carried out a large morphological comparison involving more than 2,000 chelae and similar grasping appendages from both living and extinct species. Their analysis showed that the claws of this fossil bug were strikingly different from those found in other insects. Instead, the structures more closely resembled those seen in more distant arthropods such as decapods (crabs, lobsters, shrimps, etc.) and tanaids.

New Species Named After Stray Kids

Because of its highly unusual anatomy, the insect was assigned to an entirely new genus named Carcinonepa. The name combines the Latinized Greek term for "crab" (carcino-) with nepa, referencing the true water bug group Nepomorpha.

"The species name libererrantes is a Latinization of the highly successful K-pop group Stray Kids," explains Carolin Haug. "The name seemed fitting because the posture of the fossil's chelae strongly resembles the group's trademark pose. Stray Kids, I should add, is the favorite band of one of the paper's authors, Fenja Haug."

Based on preserved physical traits, the researchers classified Carcinonepa libererrantes as part of the true water bugs (Nepomorpha) within the broader true bug group (Heteroptera). Aside from its dramatic claws, the insect's body shares similarities with modern Gelastocoridae, also called toad bugs, which are land-dwelling predators.

A Tiny Predator From a Cretaceous Forest

"The morphology of C. libererrantes suggests that this species had a similar lifestyle," observes Carolin Haug. "We can imagine it living in a Cretaceous forest, probably near the coast."

Researchers believe the oversized chelae on its front legs were likely used to seize and hold small insect prey as it hunted through the ancient coastal environment.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a 100-million-year-old insect with unique crab-like claws, representing a rare evolutionary adaptation. This finding expands understanding of insect evolution and biodiversity in the Cretaceous period, supported by advanced imaging technology and detailed morphological analysis. The discovery has broad scientific significance and enriches knowledge of ancient ecosystems.
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists studied amber from Myanmar that is 100 million years old. This amber preserves fossils from a time when dinosaurs lived, showing many ancient plants and animals.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered a new type of bug inside this amber. The bug’s front legs had big claws that look like crab pincers. These claw-like parts, called chelae, are very rare in insects. The team used special 3D imaging to study the bug’s body in detail and found its claws were different from other insects but similar to crabs and lobsters.

**Impact** – This bug is the fourth known insect to have evolved crab-like claws on its own. It shows that insects can develop unusual body parts independently. The bug was named Carcinonepa libererrantes, inspired by the K-pop group Stray Kids because of the claw’s pose. This discovery helps scientists learn more about insect evolution and the variety of life in ancient forests.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will keep studying these kinds of fossils to understand how insects lived and adapted millions of years ago. This could reveal more about ancient ecosystems and how creatures survived during the time of dinosaurs.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A 100-million-year-old bug with crab-like claws reveals a rare example of unique insect evolution and adds to our knowledge of ancient life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un insecte vieux de 100 millions d’années découvert avec des pinces semblables à celles d’un crabe
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques ont étudié de l’ambre du Myanmar datant de 100 millions d’années. Cet ambre contient des fossiles d’un temps où vivaient les dinosaures, montrant de nombreuses plantes et animaux anciens.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert un nouvel insecte dans cet ambre. Ses pattes avant avaient de grandes pinces ressemblant à celles d’un crabe. Ces parties appelées chélae sont très rares chez les insectes. L’équipe a utilisé une imagerie 3D spéciale pour examiner le corps de l’insecte en détail et a trouvé que ses pinces étaient différentes des autres insectes mais proches de celles des crabes et homards.

**Impact** – Cet insecte est le quatrième connu à avoir développé indépendamment des pinces semblables à celles d’un crabe. Cela montre que les insectes peuvent créer des parties du corps inhabituelles tout seuls. L’insecte a été nommé Carcinonepa libererrantes, en référence au groupe K-pop Stray Kids à cause de la posture des pinces. Cette découverte aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre l’évolution des insectes et la diversité de la vie dans les forêts anciennes.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les scientifiques continueront d’étudier ce type de fossiles pour comprendre comment les insectes vivaient et s’adaptaient il y a des millions d’années. Cela pourrait révéler plus sur les écosystèmes anciens et la survie des êtres vivants à l’époque des dinosaures.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un insecte vieux de 100 millions d’années avec des pinces de crabe montre un exemple rare d’évolution unique chez les insectes et enrichit nos connaissances sur la vie ancienne.
articles.personality_title_es: Descubren insecto de 100 millones de años con garras similares a las de un cangrejo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos estudiaron ámbar de Myanmar que tiene 100 millones de años. Este ámbar conserva fósiles de la época de los dinosaurios, mostrando muchas plantas y animales antiguos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron un nuevo tipo de insecto dentro de este ámbar. Sus patas delanteras tenían grandes garras parecidas a las pinzas de un cangrejo. Estas partes, llamadas quelas, son muy raras en insectos. El equipo usó imágenes 3D especiales para estudiar el cuerpo del insecto y descubrió que sus garras eran diferentes a las de otros insectos, pero similares a las de cangrejos y langostas.

**Impacto** – Este insecto es el cuarto conocido que desarrolló garras parecidas a las de un cangrejo de forma independiente. Esto muestra que los insectos pueden crear partes del cuerpo inusuales por sí mismos. El insecto fue nombrado Carcinonepa libererrantes, inspirado en el grupo de K-pop Stray Kids por la postura de sus garras. Este hallazgo ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor la evolución de los insectos y la variedad de vida en bosques antiguos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando estos fósiles para aprender cómo vivían y se adaptaban los insectos hace millones de años. Esto podría revelar más sobre ecosistemas antiguos y cómo sobrevivieron los seres vivos en la época de los dinosaurios.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un insecto de 100 millones de años con garras similares a las de un cangrejo muestra un raro ejemplo de evolución única en insectos y amplía nuestro conocimiento sobre la vida antigua.
articles.image_prompt: An intricately detailed drawing of a small ancient true bug with large, crab-like clawed front legs perched on a piece of amber, set against a softly lit background evoking a Cretaceous coastal forest, using warm, natural earth tones and muted greens to highlight the unique fusion of insect and crustacean features.
